skill.name: pptx-posters
skill.description: Create PowerPoint-oriented research posters with HTML and CSS templates, then export them to PDF or PPTX when that format is explicitly required.
skill.summary: Build HTML/CSS research posters for PDF or PPTX delivery.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 417 issues, including a critical heuristic, but review found that most hits are Markdown examples, CSS class names, LaTeX snippets, and poster terminology. No prompt injection, credential access, obfuscation, data exfiltration, or active network code was found. Risk is medium because the skill is Bash-enabled and documents local file creation, browser export, and conversion commands that should be reviewed before execution.

medium_findings.title: Bash-Enabled Export Workflow Requires Command Review
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill declares Bash access and documents local commands for creating figure folders, copying templates, opening HTML files, exporting PDFs with Chrome, and converting PDFs to PPTX with LibreOffice. These commands are legitimate for poster production, but they should be reviewed and kept to trusted local files to avoid shell-injection or unintended file operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash permission and command examples are explicit, and the workflow can write and convert local files. The intent is legitimate poster export, so the concern is operational caution rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Alerts Are Poster Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The certificate or key-file alerts map to CSS and documentation phrases such as key-finding and Key methodology. No private keys, certificates, tokens, or credential material were found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible CSS selectors, placeholder poster content, and a class-name table. There is no credential syntax or secret-bearing file content.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak-cryptography alerts occur in poster design, HTML, LaTeX, and checklist text. Review found no cryptographic functions, hash algorithms, encryption routines, or security-sensitive authentication logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines contain poster layout examples, print-quality checklist items, and design prose. No cryptographic implementation is present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Reference Links Only
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs are accessibility-testing resources, a sample DOI QR-code target, and a creator website mention. The files do not contain code that makes network requests or sends user data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in documentation and example poster content, not in fetch, curl, script, or telemetry code. The K-Dense mention is promotional but not an exfiltration mechanism.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Targeted review found no text claiming to override system instructions, skip security analysis, assert pre-approval, or impersonate administrator authority. No prompt-injection finding is confirmed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The searched trigger phrases were absent, and the reviewed ending section contains a product suggestion rather than an instruction override. Hidden injection in uninspected prose is unlikely but cannot be ruled out with absolute certainty.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Shell Commands in Markdown Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The Markdown examples include local shell commands such as mkdir, cp, open, xdg-open, google-chrome, libreoffice, grep, pdfinfo, pdffonts, pdfimages, ls, and gs. These are common poster build and quality-check commands, but marketplace users should treat them as commands to inspect before running.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are real and numerous, but they are documentation for local poster workflows rather than hidden executable payloads. Risk depends on how an agent instantiates user-provided values into commands.

content.value_statement: Research posters often need strict sizing, readable layouts, and editable delivery formats. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through HTML/CSS poster creation with PDF and PPTX export paths.
actual_capabilities: Provides a 36 by 48 inch HTML/CSS poster template with header, columns, content blocks, and footer sections.
Guides PDF export through browser print settings and optional headless Chrome commands.
Documents PPTX conversion options through LibreOffice or direct python-pptx construction.
Gives poster-specific rules for large text, simple graphics, visual hierarchy, and overflow prevention.
Includes quality checks for page size, fonts, images, accessibility, printing, and presentation readiness.
Supplies reference guidance for content structure, design principles, and layout patterns.
limitations: Use this skill only when a user explicitly needs PPTX, PowerPoint, or HTML-based poster output.
It does not generate LaTeX posters and points standard poster requests toward a LaTeX-focused workflow.
PPTX output may require installed tools such as LibreOffice, Chrome, or python-pptx.
Image generation and scientific figure creation are delegated to separate tools or skills.

use_cases.title: Conference Poster Delivery
use_cases.description: Create a conference-ready poster that can be exported as a PDF and converted to PPTX for editable submission.

use_cases.title: Defense or Lab Presentation
use_cases.description: Build a readable poster with strong visual hierarchy, limited text, and clear methods and results sections.

use_cases.title: Reusable HTML Poster Template
use_cases.description: Adapt a web-based poster layout for project showcases, science events, or internal research reviews.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic PPTX poster
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with a known topic
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PowerPoint-ready research poster about [topic]. Use the HTML/CSS poster template, include Introduction, Methods, Results, and Conclusions, and prepare it for PDF export.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare conference poster content
prompt_templates.scenario: User has research notes and conference constraints
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn my research notes into a 36 by 48 inch PPTX poster draft. Keep total text under 800 words, plan 3 to 5 visuals, and make all section headings readable from a distance.

prompt_templates.title: Convert HTML poster to PPTX workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs an editable PowerPoint file after HTML design
prompt_templates.prompt: Create the HTML poster first, then give me a safe local workflow to export it to PDF and convert it to PPTX. List any required tools before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Audit and refine poster before submission
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before printing or uploading
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this poster for overflow, figure readability, font sizes, accessibility, print settings, and PPTX conversion risks. Recommend precise layout and content changes.

output_examples.input: Create a PPTX poster for a machine learning conference paper.
output_examples.output: A structured poster plan with title, author area, three-column layout, visual slots, concise section text, export steps, and quality checks.

output_examples.input: Make my poster readable from six feet away.
output: Simplified graphics with three to four elements each.
Larger title, author, section, and body text sizes.
Reduced section count and a shorter total word budget.

output_examples.input: I need a PowerPoint version after designing in HTML.
output_examples.output: A PDF-first export workflow with a PPTX conversion path, tool prerequisites, and warnings to inspect commands before running them.

best_practices: Confirm that the user explicitly needs PPTX or PowerPoint before using this skill.
Keep poster graphics simple, with large labels, high contrast, and generous white space.
Preview the exported PDF and PPTX at reduced scale before printing or submission.
anti_patterns: Do not use this workflow for ordinary poster requests when a LaTeX poster is more appropriate.
Do not paste untrusted user text directly into shell commands without reviewing quoting and paths.
Do not cram many sections, small labels, or complex workflows into one poster graphic.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when the poster must be HTML-based, editable in PowerPoint, or delivered as a PPTX file.

faq.question: Can it create a standard academic poster?
faq.answer: Yes, but standard poster requests should usually use a LaTeX poster workflow unless PPTX is required.

faq.question: Does it directly generate a PowerPoint file?
faq.answer: It guides HTML poster creation first, then documents conversion options for PPTX output.

faq.question: What external tools may be needed?
faq.answer: Chrome, Firefox, LibreOffice, or python-pptx may be needed depending on the export path.

faq.question: Does it include design guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for poster text limits, image readability, layout, visual hierarchy, and quality checks.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it uses Bash-enabled local export commands that users should inspect before execution.
